case_name: Cyanide removal from contaminated groundwater for municipal drinking water supply
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for cyanide-contaminated groundwater intended for drinking water production.

input_data.scenario_background: A small town relies on groundwater wells located downgradient of a former metal-finishing area and has detected cyanide in raw water. The utility needs a clear and proven treatment approach suitable for drinking water production and prefers established oxidation-based treatment rather than novel or highly specialized systems. The utility can manage chemical dosing and filtration but does not want unnecessarily high-energy treatment.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should comply with drinking water requirements for cyanide and provide stable potable water quality.
secondary_goals: maintain moderate chemical and operational complexity
provide reliable treatment for a relatively low-turbidity groundwater source

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: limited but manageable handling of backwash water and minor solids is available; hazardous concentrate streams are undesirable
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: stable and conservative operation is required for continuous municipal supply

core_unit_functions.alkaline_pH_control: maintain conditions that suppress hydrogen cyanide volatilization and support controlled oxidation
core_unit_functions.chlorine_or_hypochlorite_oxidation: oxidize cyanide to less harmful compounds under established treatment practice
core_unit_functions.contact_tank: provide sufficient reaction time for cyanide destruction
core_unit_functions.granular_media_filtration: remove any formed solids and provide conventional polishing
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection: ensure microbiological safety of finished drinking water

applicability_rationale: cyanide is the single explicit target contaminant and oxidation is a conventional treatment approach
groundwater has low turbidity and low DOC, making chemical oxidation more straightforward
chlorine-based oxidation is widely known and more consistent with a proven technology preference than advanced specialty systems
the utility can manage chemical feed and contact time control within moderate operational capability
potential_risks: pH must be controlled to minimize hydrogen cyanide release during treatment
insufficient oxidant dose or contact time can leave residual cyanide
care is needed to manage disinfection by-product formation and avoid overchlorination

core_unit_functions.alkaline_pH_control: maintain safe cyanide speciation and suitable oxidation conditions
core_unit_functions.potassium_permanganate_or_ozone_oxidatio: chemically oxidize cyanide using an established oxidant alternative
core_unit_functions.granular_media_filtration: polish water and capture any particulate byproducts

applicability_rationale: oxidation remains the central proven removal mechanism for cyanide
this option can be feasible where the utility wants an alternative to free chlorine as the primary oxidant
the groundwater matrix is relatively clean, so oxidant demand should remain moderate
potential_risks: ozone systems increase equipment complexity and operator attention
permanganate may require careful dose control to avoid residual color or excess manganese
cyanide destruction performance must be validated for the specific oxidant and water chemistry

core_unit_functions.air_stripping_with_offgas_control: transfer volatile hydrogen cyanide from water under controlled conditions
core_unit_functions.oxidation_of_offgas_or_stripped_stream: destroy captured cyanide in the gas treatment or secondary treatment stage
core_unit_functions.final_disinfection: ensure microbiological safety of produced water

applicability_rationale: volatilization can be technically relevant for cyanide species under some conditions
this option may be considered when oxidation alone is difficult or when specific site configurations already include air handling
it is not preferred for a simple drinking water case because direct oxidation is usually more straightforward
potential_risks: hydrogen cyanide is highly hazardous, so off-gas capture and worker safety are critical
process is sensitive to pH and can increase safety management burden
less aligned with conservative municipal drinking water operation than liquid-phase oxidation

core_unit_functions.anion_exchange: remove cyanide-related anionic species under favorable water chemistry conditions
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: polish effluent and retain any resin fines

applicability_rationale: ion exchange can be a niche option depending on cyanide speciation and competing ions
the relatively moderate sulfate and chloride concentrations may still allow some feasibility assessment
however, it is less broadly supported as the primary mainstream solution than oxidation for this case
potential_risks: selectivity may be reduced by competing anions and influent chemistry changes
spent regenerant or waste brine handling can be problematic for cyanide-bearing residuals
breakthrough monitoring is essential to prevent finished-water exceedance

core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: provide broad dissolved contaminant rejection including cyanide-related species
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust permeate chemistry for distribution system compatibility

applicability_rationale: reverse osmosis is technically capable of reducing many dissolved contaminants
it may be considered when multiple dissolved contaminants also require simultaneous removal
for a single cyanide problem in otherwise moderate-quality groundwater, this is more complex than necessary
potential_risks: concentrate management for cyanide-bearing reject stream is a major disadvantage
higher energy and membrane maintenance burden conflict with a proven simple treatment preference
post-treatment stabilization is needed before distribution

preferred_rank_pattern: oxidation_based treatment with alkaline control should rank first for cyanide in groundwater under proven-technology preference
alternative oxidants may rank below chlorination-based oxidation but above niche separation processes
high-pressure membrane options should not rank first for a single-contaminant moderate-constraint case

must_reflect: preference for established and conservative treatment practice
moderate municipal operational capability with chemical dosing and contact tanks
avoidance of unnecessarily complex or high-energy treatment trains

must_contain: the role of oxidation in cyanide destruction
the importance of pH control to limit hydrogen cyanide volatilization and maintain treatment safety
why conventional oxidation is more appropriate than reverse osmosis for this straightforward case

should_mention: need for adequate oxidant dose and contact time
residuals or concentrate management concerns for non-oxidation alternatives
